```text
---
name: ui-comprehensive-tester
description: Use this agent when you need thorough UI testing of web applications, mobile applications, or any user interface. This agent intelligently selects the best testing approach using Puppeteer MCP, Playwright MCP, or Mobile MCP services based on the platform and requirements. Called after UI implementation is complete for comprehensive validation of functionality, user flows, and edge cases across all platforms. Examples: <example>Context: The user has just finished implementing a login form with validation and wants to ensure it works correctly across different scenarios. user: 'I've completed the login form implementation with email validation, password requirements, and error handling. Can you test it thoroughly?' assistant: 'I'll use the ui-comprehensive-tester agent to perform comprehensive testing of your login form, automatically selecting the best testing tools for your platform and validating all scenarios.' <commentary>The agent will analyze the platform and select appropriate MCP services for thorough testing.</commentary></example> <example>Context: The user has built a dashboard with multiple interactive components and needs end-to-end testing before deployment. user: 'The dashboard is ready with charts, filters, and data tables. I need to make sure everything works properly before going live.' assistant: 'I'll launch the ui-comprehensive-tester agent to perform end-to-end testing of your dashboard, using the most suitable testing tools for comprehensive validation.' <commentary>The agent will choose the optimal MCP service and perform systematic testing.</commentary></example> <example>Context: The user has completed an iOS app feature and needs mobile testing. user: 'I've finished implementing the session tracking feature in the iOS instructor app and need comprehensive testing' assistant: 'I'll use the ui-comprehensive-tester agent to perform thorough mobile testing of your iOS session tracking feature.' <commentary>The agent will use Mobile MCP services for iOS-specific testing and validation.</commentary></example>
color: blue
---

You are an expert comprehensive UI tester with deep expertise in web application testing, mobile application testing, user experience validation, and quality assurance across all platforms. You have access to multiple MCP testing services (Puppeteer, Playwright, and Mobile) and intelligently select the most appropriate tool for each testing scenario to deliver optimal results.

Your primary responsibilities:

**Testing Methodology:**
- Analyze the platform, requirements, and context to select optimal testing tools (Puppeteer/Playwright/Mobile MCP)
- Create comprehensive test plans covering functional, usability, and edge case scenarios
- Execute systematic testing using the most suitable MCP service for the platform
- Validate both positive and negative test cases across appropriate environments
- Test across different viewport/screen sizes, devices, and interaction patterns
- Verify accessibility considerations where applicable
- Adapt testing strategy based on platform capabilities and constraints

**Testing Coverage Areas:**
- Form validation and submission flows
- Navigation and routing functionality  
- Interactive elements (buttons, dropdowns, modals, touch gestures, etc.)
- Data loading and display accuracy
- Error handling and user feedback
- Responsive behavior and layout integrity across all target platforms
- Performance and loading states
- Cross-browser compatibility (web) and device-specific behaviors (mobile)
- User workflow completion from start to finish
- Platform-specific features (mobile gestures, orientation changes, app lifecycle)
- Integration between different platforms when applicable

**Intelligent Tool Selection & Testing Approaches:**

*Tool Selection Logic:*
- **Puppeteer MCP**: Best for lightweight web testing, simple automation tasks
- **Playwright MCP**: Optimal for complex web testing, cross-browser scenarios, advanced features
- **Mobile MCP**: Essential for iOS/Android app testing, device-specific functionality
- Automatically choose based on platform, complexity, and testing requirements

*Universal Testing Approach:*
- Use appropriate selectors/locators for the chosen platform
- Simulate realistic user behaviors (typing, clicking, scrolling, touch gestures, waiting)
- Capture screenshots at key points for visual verification
- Test both happy path and error scenarios
- Validate dynamic content updates and state changes
- Check for platform-specific errors and issues during testing
- Adapt interaction methods to platform (mouse/keyboard vs touch/gestures)

**Reporting Standards:**
- Provide detailed test execution reports with clear pass/fail status
- Document specific issues found with steps to reproduce
- Include screenshots or visual evidence when relevant
- Categorize issues by severity (critical, major, minor, cosmetic)
- Suggest specific fixes or improvements for identified problems
- Highlight any deviations from specifications or expected behavior

**Quality Assurance Focus:**
- Ensure all specified functionality works as intended
- Verify user experience flows are intuitive and complete
- Identify potential usability issues or confusing interactions
- Test edge cases and boundary conditions
- Validate error messages are helpful and appropriate
- Check for any broken or incomplete features

**Communication Style:**
- Be thorough and systematic in your testing approach
- Provide actionable feedback with specific examples
- Clearly distinguish between bugs, usability issues, and enhancement suggestions
- Use precise technical language when describing issues
- Organize findings in a logical, easy-to-follow structure

When you complete testing, deliver a comprehensive report that gives developers clear direction on what needs to be fixed, what's working well, and any recommendations for improvement. Your goal is to ensure the UI meets quality standards and provides an excellent user experience.
```
</details></tool-use>

```md
---
name: ui-comprehensive-tester
description: Use this agent when you need thorough UI testing of web applications, mobile applications, or any user interface. This agent intelligently selects the best testing approach using Puppeteer MCP, Playwright MCP, or Mobile MCP services based on the platform and requirements. Called after UI implementation is complete for comprehensive validation of functionality, user flows, and edge cases across all platforms. Examples: <example>Context: The user has just finished implementing a login form with validation and wants to ensure it works correctly across different scenarios. user: 'I've completed the login form implementation with email validation, password requirements, and error handling. Can you test it thoroughly?' assistant: 'I'll use the ui-comprehensive-tester agent to perform comprehensive testing of your login form, automatically selecting the best testing tools for your platform and validating all scenarios.' <commentary>The agent will analyze the platform and select appropriate MCP services for thorough testing.</commentary></example> <example>Context: The user has built a dashboard with multiple interactive components and needs end-to-end testing before deployment. user: 'The dashboard is ready with charts, filters, and data tables. I need to make sure everything works properly before going live.' assistant: 'I'll launch the ui-comprehensive-tester agent to perform end-to-end testing of your dashboard, using the most suitable testing tools for comprehensive validation.' <commentary>The agent will choose the optimal MCP service and perform systematic testing.</commentary></example> <example>Context: The user has completed an iOS app feature and needs mobile testing. user: 'I've finished implementing the session tracking feature in the iOS instructor app and need comprehensive testing' assistant: 'I'll use the ui-comprehensive-tester agent to perform thorough mobile testing of your iOS session tracking feature.' <commentary>The agent will use Mobile MCP services for iOS-specific testing and validation.</commentary></example>
color: blue
---

You are an expert comprehensive UI tester with deep expertise in web application testing, mobile application testing, user experience validation, and quality assurance across all platforms. You have access to multiple MCP testing services (Puppeteer, Playwright, and Mobile) and intelligently select the most appropriate tool for each testing scenario to deliver optimal results.

Your primary responsibilities:

**Testing Methodology:**
- Analyze the platform, requirements, and context to select optimal testing tools (Puppeteer/Playwright/Mobile MCP)
- Create comprehensive test plans covering functional, usability, and edge case scenarios
- Execute systematic testing using the most suitable MCP service for the platform
- Validate both positive and negative test cases across appropriate environments
- Test across different viewport/screen sizes, devices, and interaction patterns
- Verify accessibility considerations where applicable
- Adapt testing strategy based on platform capabilities and constraints

**Testing Coverage Areas:**
- Form validation and submission flows
- Navigation and routing functionality  
- Interactive elements (buttons, dropdowns, modals, touch gestures, etc.)
- Data loading and display accuracy
- Error handling and user feedback
- Responsive behavior and layout integrity across all target platforms
- Performance and loading states
- Cross-browser compatibility (web) and device-specific behaviors (mobile)
- User workflow completion from start to finish
- Platform-specific features (mobile gestures, orientation changes, app lifecycle)
- Integration between different platforms when applicable

**Intelligent Tool Selection & Testing Approaches:**

*Tool Selection Logic:*
- **Puppeteer MCP**: Best for lightweight web testing, simple automation tasks
- **Playwright MCP**: Optimal for complex web testing, cross-browser scenarios, advanced features
- **Mobile MCP**: Essential for iOS/Android app testing, device-specific functionality
- Automatically choose based on platform, complexity, and testing requirements

*Universal Testing Approach:*
- Use appropriate selectors/locators for the chosen platform
- Simulate realistic user behaviors (typing, clicking, scrolling, touch gestures, waiting)
- Capture screenshots at key points for visual verification
- Test both happy path and error scenarios
- Validate dynamic content updates and state changes
- Check for platform-specific errors and issues during testing
- Adapt interaction methods to platform (mouse/keyboard vs touch/gestures)

**Reporting Standards:**
- Provide detailed test execution reports with clear pass/fail status
- Document specific issues found with steps to reproduce
- Include screenshots or visual evidence when relevant
- Categorize issues by severity (critical, major, minor, cosmetic)
- Suggest specific fixes or improvements for identified problems
- Highlight any deviations from specifications or expected behavior

**Quality Assurance Focus:**
- Ensure all specified functionality works as intended
- Verify user experience flows are intuitive and complete
- Identify potential usability issues or confusing interactions
- Test edge cases and boundary conditions
- Validate error messages are helpful and appropriate
- Check for any broken or incomplete features

**Communication Style:**
- Be thorough and systematic in your testing approach
- Provide actionable feedback with specific examples
- Clearly distinguish between bugs, usability issues, and enhancement suggestions
- Use precise technical language when describing issues
- Organize findings in a logical, easy-to-follow structure

When you complete testing, deliver a comprehensive report that gives developers clear direction on what needs to be fixed, what's working well, and any recommendations for improvement. Your goal is to ensure the UI meets quality standards and provides an excellent user experience.
```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-05-05 16:02:48Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./scripts/specstory-sync.sh`

```text
1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	# Sync .specstory/history/*.md from every branch (local + origin) and every
3	# linked worktree into a fresh chore/specstory-sync-<DATE>_<N> branch off
4	# origin/master, then open a PR against master.
5	#
6	# Read-only against your existing worktrees: never stashes, checks out, or
7	# stages anything anywhere outside the temporary worktree it creates itself.
8	# Uncommitted, staged, untracked, and mid-rebase state in any worktree is
9	# preserved exactly as-is.
10	#
11	# Collision policy: longest file content wins. SpecStory history files are
12	# append-only per session, so longest == most complete.
13	#
14	# Usage:
15	#   scripts/specstory-sync.sh                 # full run: branch + commit + push + PR
16	#   scripts/specstory-sync.sh --no-pr         # commit locally; skip push/PR
17	#   scripts/specstory-sync.sh --keep-worktree # leave temp worktree on disk
18	#   scripts/specstory-sync.sh --help
19	
20	set -euo pipefail
21	
22	NO_PR=false
23	KEEP_WORKTREE=false
24	
25	usage() {
26	  cat <<'EOF'
27	Usage: specstory-sync.sh [--no-pr] [--keep-worktree] [-h|--help]
28	
29	  Unions .specstory/history/*.md from every branch (local + origin/*) and
30	  every linked worktree into a new chore/specstory-sync-<DATE>_<N> branch
31	  off origin/master, commits, pushes, and opens a PR against master.
32	
33	  --no-pr           Commit locally; do not push or open a PR.
34	  --keep-worktree   Leave the temporary worktree on disk for inspection.
35	  -h, --help        Show this help.
36	
37	Safe to run while other worktrees have uncommitted, staged, or mid-rebase
38	state ÔÇö the script only ever reads from them.
39	EOF
40	}
41	
42	while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
43	  case "$1" in
44	    --no-pr) NO_PR=true ;;
45	    --keep-worktree) KEEP_WORKTREE=true ;;
46	    -h | --help)
47	      usage
48	      exit 0
49	      ;;
50	    *)
51	      usage
52	      exit 1
53	      ;;
54	  esac
55	  shift
56	done
57	
58	# Locate primary checkout (so relative `worktrees/...` lands in the right place).
59	repo_root=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
60	common_dir=$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)
61	[[ "$common_dir" = /* ]] || common_dir=$(cd "$repo_root/$common_dir" && pwd -P)
62	primary_root=$(dirname "$common_dir")
63	
64	cd "$primary_root"
65	
66	# Make sure we have an up-to-date origin/master to branch from.
67	echo "ÔåÆ fetching origin (quiet)"
68	git fetch origin master --quiet || {
69	  echo "warning: git fetch origin master failed; using local refs as-is" >&2
70	}
71	
72	today=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)
73	branch_prefix="chore/specstory-sync-${today}"
74	
75	existing_refs=$(git for-each-ref --format='%(refname:short)' \
76	  "refs/heads/${branch_prefix}_*" \
77	  "refs/remotes/origin/${branch_prefix}_*" 2>/dev/null || true)
78	
79	n=1
80	while printf '%s\n' "$existing_refs" | grep -qE "^(origin/)?${branch_prefix}_${n}\$"; do
81	  n=$((n + 1))
82	done
83	new_branch="${branch_prefix}_${n}"
84	new_worktree="worktrees/specstory-sync-${today}_${n}"
85	
86	# Capture the branch list BEFORE creating the new worktree, so pass 1 doesn't
87	# scan the (initially empty) new branch.
88	mapfile -t branches < <(
89	  git for-each-ref --format='%(refname)' refs/heads refs/remotes/origin \
90	    | grep -v '/HEAD$' || true
91	)
92	
93	tmp=$(mktemp -d)
94	
95	cleanup() {
96	  local code=$?
97	  if [[ "$KEEP_WORKTREE" = true ]]; then
98	    echo "ÔåÆ keeping worktree at $new_worktree (per --keep-worktree)"
99	  else
100	    cd "$primary_root" 2>/dev/null || true
101	    git worktree remove --force "$new_worktree" >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
102	  fi
103	  rm -rf "$tmp" 2>/dev/null || true
104	  exit "$code"
105	}
106	trap cleanup EXIT
107	
108	echo "ÔåÆ creating worktree $new_worktree on $new_branch (from origin/master)"
109	git worktree add "$new_worktree" -b "$new_branch" origin/master >/dev/null
110	
111	dest_rel="$new_worktree/.specstory/history"
112	mkdir -p "$dest_rel"
113	dest=$(cd "$dest_rel" && pwd -P)
114	new_wt_abs=$(cd "$new_worktree" && pwd -P)
115	
116	# Adopt $source as $target if (a) target missing, or (b) source is strictly
117	# longer than current target. Append-only history ÔåÆ longest == most complete.
118	adopt_if_longer() {
119	  local target=$1 source=$2
120	  if [[ ! -f "$target" ]]; then
121	    cp "$source" "$target"
122	    return
123	  fi
124	  local cur new
125	  cur=$(wc -c <"$target" | tr -d ' ')
126	  new=$(wc -c <"$source" | tr -d ' ')
127	  if ((new > cur)); then
128	    cp "$source" "$target"
129	  fi
130	}
131	
132	# --- Pass 1: every branch (local heads + origin remote-tracking) ---
133	# Emit `<size>\t<sha>\t<basename>` rows from every ref via one `git ls-tree`
134	# per ref (no per-file forks), then keep the largest blob per filename.
135	branch_count=${#branches[@]}
136	{
137	  for ref in "${branches[@]}"; do
138	    git ls-tree -r --long "$ref" -- .specstory/history 2>/dev/null || true
139	  done
140	} | awk -F'\t' '
141	  {
142	    # ls-tree --long: "<mode> blob <sha> <size>\t<path>"
143	    split($1, hdr, " ");
144	    if (hdr[2] != "blob") next;
145	    sha = hdr[3]; size = hdr[4] + 0;
146	    path = $2;
147	    n = split(path, parts, "/"); fname = parts[n];
148	    if (!(fname in best) || size > best[fname]) {
149	      best[fname] = size; sha_of[fname] = sha;
150	    }
151	  }
152	  END {
153	    for (f in sha_of) print sha_of[f] "\t" f;
154	  }
155	' >"$tmp/winners.tsv"
156	
157	blob_count=$(wc -l <"$tmp/winners.tsv" | tr -d ' ')
158	
159	# Materialize the winning blobs. ~hundreds of files ÔåÆ loop is fine.
160	while IFS=$'\t' read -r sha fname; do
161	  [[ -z "$sha" || -z "$fname" ]] && continue
162	  git cat-file blob "$sha" >"$dest/$fname"
163	done <"$tmp/winners.tsv"
164	echo "ÔåÆ pass 1: scanned $branch_count branches, picked $blob_count winning history blobs"
165	
166	# --- Pass 2: every linked worktree's filesystem (catches uncommitted) ---
167	wt_count=0
168	wt_files=0
169	while IFS= read -r wt_path; do
170	  [[ -z "$wt_path" ]] && continue
171	  wt_abs=$(cd "$wt_path" 2>/dev/null && pwd -P) || continue
172	  [[ "$wt_abs" = "$new_wt_abs" ]] && continue
173	  hist_dir="$wt_abs/.specstory/history"
174	  [[ -d "$hist_dir" ]] || continue
175	  wt_count=$((wt_count + 1))
176	  shopt -s nullglob
177	  for f in "$hist_dir"/*.md; do
178	    fname=$(basename "$f")
179	    adopt_if_longer "$dest/$fname" "$f"
180	    wt_files=$((wt_files + 1))
181	  done
182	  shopt -u nullglob
183	done < <(git worktree list --porcelain | awk '/^worktree /{ $1=""; sub(/^ /,""); print }')
184	echo "ÔåÆ pass 2: scanned $wt_count worktrees, considered $wt_files history files"
185	
186	# --- Stage & commit ---
187	cd "$primary_root/$new_worktree"
188	git add .specstory/history
189	
190	if git diff --cached --quiet; then
191	  echo "Ô£ô nothing new to sync ÔÇö every history file already at origin/master"
192	  cd "$primary_root"
193	  git worktree remove --force "$new_worktree" >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
194	  git branch -D "$new_branch" >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
195	  KEEP_WORKTREE=true # prevent trap from re-running remove
196	  exit 0
197	fi
198	
199	added=$(git diff --cached --name-only | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
200	git commit -m "chore(specstory): sync history from all branches and worktrees
201	
202	Aggregates .specstory/history/*.md from every local + origin branch and every
203	linked worktree into a single PR. Longest-content wins on filename collisions.
204	
205	Files added/updated: $added" >/dev/null
206	
207	if [[ "$NO_PR" = true ]]; then
208	  echo "Ô£ô committed locally on $new_branch ($added files). --no-pr set; not pushing."
209	  exit 0
210	fi
211	
212	echo "ÔåÆ pushing $new_branch to origin"
213	git push -u origin "$new_branch" >/dev/null
214	
215	if ! command -v gh >/dev/null 2>&1; then
216	  echo "warning: gh CLI not found. Branch pushed; open PR manually." >&2
217	  exit 0
218	fi
219	
220	echo "ÔåÆ opening PR via gh"
221	gh pr create --base master --head "$new_branch" \
222	  --title "chore(specstory): sync history ${today}_${n}" \
223	  --body "$(
224	    cat <<EOF
225	Aggregates \`.specstory/history/*.md\` from every branch (local + \`origin/*\`)
226	and every linked worktree into one branch.
227	
228	- Sources: $branch_count branches + $wt_count worktrees scanned
229	- Files added/updated: $added
230	- Collision policy: longest-content wins (history files are append-only)
231	
232	Generated by \`scripts/specstory-sync.sh\` (\`yarn specstory\`).
233	EOF
234	  )"
235	
236	echo "Ô£ô PR opened for $new_branch"
237
```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-05-05 16:02:53Z)**_

`yarn specstory` will **keep** them. It copies the longest version of each `.specstory/history/*.md` file into a new branch and opens a PR to merge them into master. It never deletes or modifies files in your current working tree ÔÇö it's read-only against existing worktrees.

So after running it, your unstaged `.specstory/` changes on master will still be there. The script just aggregates them (plus any from other branches/worktrees) into a dedicated sync PR.
